purpose: BATS test suites for the two standalone validator scripts — `scripts/validate-skill.sh` and `scripts/validate-subagent.sh`. These tests verify the structural, frontmatter, content-quality, and codebase-specificity checks that Phase 5 of ultrainit runs on every generated skill and subagent before writing artifacts. They run inside Docker with a full mock environment and no real Claude API calls.

architecture.overview: Two BATS test files, each covering one validator script. Both delegate setup/teardown to a shared helper library (`tests/helpers/test_helper.bash`) that creates an isolated tmpdir, exports all required environment variables, and sources all eight project libs at once. Fixture data comes from either pre-built YAML files in `tests/fixtures/` or inline heredocs that dynamically construct edge-case inputs. The mock_claude.bash helper exists in the same helpers directory but is NOT used by these tests — the validators are pure shell scripts that never call Claude, so no mock is needed.

subdirectories.purpose: The two .bats test suites: one for validate-skill.sh (21 tests) and one for validate-subagent.sh (6 tests).
Shared infrastructure loaded by every test suite: _common_setup/_common_teardown, make_claude_envelope, and the PATH-mock for Claude.
Pre-built SKILL.md fixtures: one valid, two invalid (missing description, angle brackets in description).
Pre-built subagent fixtures: one valid (code-reviewer.md), one invalid (no frontmatter).
The scripts under test. Both validators are standalone shell scripts that accept a file path, run multi-stage checks, and exit 0 (pass/warnings) or 1 (fail).

architecture.data_flow: Each test calls `run bash scripts/validate-skill.sh <path>` or the subagent equivalent. The validator reads the target file, extracts YAML frontmatter with awk/grep/sed, applies validation rules, accumulates error/warning counts, prints a structured report to stdout, and exits. BATS captures stdout into `$output` and the exit code into `$status`. Tests then assert on `$status` (via `assert_success`/`assert_failure`) and on partial strings in `$output` (via `assert_output --partial`). No mocking is needed because the validators are pure text processors.

key_files.purpose: 21 test cases for scripts/validate-skill.sh covering: valid pass, missing/malformed frontmatter, missing description, angle brackets in description, uppercase folder name (non-kebab-case), fewer than 3 path references, description over 1024 chars, generic phrases warning, missing verification section, YAML folded/literal scalar description formats, root-level and tests/ directory references, and nonexistent file.
6 test cases for scripts/validate-subagent.sh covering: valid pass, missing frontmatter, nonexistent file, missing name field, and uppercase filename warning.

Shared setup/teardown for all test suites. Loads bats-support, bats-assert, bats-file from /usr/local/lib. Sets TEST_TMPDIR, WORK_DIR, TARGET_DIR, all budget variables, all flag variables, and color codes to empty strings. Sources all 8 project libs in dependency order so interactions (e.g. nullglob set by one lib) are caught during testing.

The validator under test. Accepts a SKILL.md path. Runs 4 check categories: structural (filename, folder kebab-case, frontmatter delimiters), frontmatter fields (name, description length/content/trigger/scope/angle-brackets), content quality (path reference count ≥ 3, verification section, generic phrase count), and outputs VERDICT: PASS / NEEDS REVISION / FAIL.

The validator under test. Accepts a .md path. Checks: file extension, filename casing, frontmatter delimiters, required name/description fields, description trigger and scope phrases, body word count ≥ 20, optional model/bypassPermissions/tools fields, path reference count, generic phrase count, and overlap with built-in Explore agent.
PATH-based mock for the claude CLI used by integration tests. Not used in tests/scripts/ (validators never call Claude), but present in helpers because it's shared across all suites.

Golden fixture that must pass all 21 validator checks. Defines what a correct SKILL.md looks like: kebab-case folder, inline description with trigger and negative scope, 5+ backtick-wrapped path references, a verification section.
Golden fixture for subagent validation. name: code-reviewer, model: sonnet, tools: Read/Glob/Grep/Bash(git:*), body with review checklist referencing 6 codebase paths.

patterns.name: Fixture-or-Heredoc test data
patterns.description: Tests that verify a documented behavior use pre-built fixtures from tests/fixtures/. Tests that probe edge cases (long description, generic phrases, missing fields) generate their own SKILL.md or .md files inline with heredocs into $TEST_TMPDIR. The rule: if the fixture would be reused or serves as a canonical example, commit it; if it's a one-off edge case, build it inline. This keeps fixtures/*.md readable and authoritative while keeping test files self-contained for edge cases.

patterns.name: run + assert_success/assert_failure + assert_output --partial
patterns.description: Every test follows the same three-step structure: (1) `run bash scripts/validate-X.sh <path>` to capture stdout and exit code, (2) `assert_success` or `assert_failure` to check exit code, (3) one or more `assert_output --partial 'expected text'` to verify specific messages appear in output. The validators always print to stdout (not stderr), so $output contains the full report. Never use assert_output alone (exact match) — always use --partial because the report contains dynamic counts and metrics.

patterns.name: Folder-as-namespace for skill fixtures
patterns.description: validate-skill.sh requires the input to be named exactly SKILL.md, and it derives the skill's name from the containing folder. So fixtures must be placed in named subdirectories: tests/fixtures/skills/valid-skill/SKILL.md, tests/fixtures/skills/invalid-skill-no-desc/SKILL.md. Dynamic tests create $TEST_TMPDIR/MySkill/SKILL.md or $TEST_TMPDIR/my-skill/SKILL.md to test folder-name validation. The folder name IS the skill name — a flat file is invalid.

patterns.name: Multi-line YAML description coverage
patterns.description: The synthesizer agent may emit descriptions as YAML folded scalars (`description: >`) or literal scalars (`description: |`) rather than inline strings. validate-skill.sh contains special parsing for both formats. The test suite has dedicated cases for each format to prevent regressions where multi-line descriptions falsely fail the 'missing description' check. These tests are the only signal that the YAML multi-line parsing works correctly.

patterns.name: Sourcing all libs in _common_setup
patterns.description: test_helper.bash sources all 8 project libs (utils, config, agent, gather, ask, research, synthesize, validate, merge) in every setup() call, even for tests that only need validate.sh. This is intentional: it detects cross-lib interactions like one lib setting bash options (nullglob, errexit) that silently break another. The cost is slower setup; the benefit is that integration-level breakage surfaces in unit tests.

patterns.name: Threshold-based VERDICT
patterns.description: Both validators use a two-tier exit: exit 1 if errors > 0 OR warnings > threshold (3 for skills, 4 for subagents); exit 0 otherwise. This means a skill with 4 warnings gets 'NEEDS REVISION' but still exits 1 (blocking). Tests verify specific error messages rather than just exit codes to confirm the right rule triggered. The thresholds differ between scripts, so a test passing for skills may not apply to subagents.

conventions.naming: Test files use snake_case matching the script under test: validate-skill.sh → validate_skill.bats, validate-subagent.sh → validate_subagent.bats. Fixture directories for skills use kebab-case folder names (valid-skill, invalid-skill-no-desc) because the validator enforces kebab-case on the folder name — so the fixtures themselves must comply. Subagent fixtures are flat .md files (valid-subagent.md, invalid-subagent.md).
conventions.imports: Each .bats file loads only `../helpers/test_helper` — a single relative path. test_helper.bash then loads all bats libraries from absolute /usr/local/lib paths (Docker-installed). No relative paths to bats libraries.
conventions.error_handling: The validators exit 1 on any error (missing file, missing frontmatter) and print an ERROR: prefixed line before exiting. Tests assert_failure and assert_output --partial 'ERROR' for these cases. Warnings use a 'WARNING:' prefix and do NOT immediately exit — they accumulate toward the warning threshold. Tests for warnings use assert_success (exit 0) + assert_output --partial for the warning text.
other: Color codes are set to empty strings in test_helper.bash so validator output is plain text — prevents ANSI escape codes from breaking assert_output --partial matching.
Dynamic test fixtures always write to $TEST_TMPDIR subdirectories, never to PROJECT_ROOT or SCRIPT_DIR — teardown cleans up TEST_TMPDIR only.
Tests use single-quoted heredocs (<<'EOF') to prevent variable expansion in fixture content, which is critical when fixture YAML contains $ or backtick characters.

The VERDICT line is always the last line of validator output: 'VERDICT: PASS', 'VERDICT: FAIL', or 'VERDICT: NEEDS REVISION' — tests can reliably assert_output --partial 'VERDICT: PASS' without worrying about line ordering.

internal: tests/helpers/mock_claude.bash (shared mock, not used here but co-located)

external_key.name: bats-core
external_key.purpose: Test runner — executes .bats files, provides @test blocks, run helper, $status/$output variables

external_key.name: bats-support
external_key.purpose: Output formatting for assertion failures — loaded from /usr/local/lib/bats-support/load

external_key.name: bats-assert
external_key.purpose: Assertion helpers: assert_success, assert_failure, assert_output --partial — loaded from /usr/local/lib/bats-assert/load

external_key.name: bats-file
external_key.purpose: File assertion helpers (assert_file_exists etc.) — loaded from /usr/local/lib/bats-file/load, available but not heavily used in scripts/ tests

external_key.name: Docker (Dockerfile.test)
external_key.purpose: Provides the isolated environment where bats and all /usr/local/lib dependencies are pre-installed. Tests are not expected to run outside the container.

external_key.name: awk, grep, sed
external_key.purpose: Used inside the validator scripts themselves for YAML frontmatter parsing — not test dependencies, but required on the host where validators run

domain_terms.meaning: The specific filename the skill validator requires. The containing folder name becomes the skill's canonical name. A skill is a Claude Code prompt-fragment that fires on user-invocable slash commands.

YAML block delimited by --- lines at the top of a markdown file. Contains name, description, and optionally model/tools/bypassPermissions fields. validate-skill.sh and validate-subagent.sh parse this with awk to find the closing ---.
The final line of validator output: PASS (exit 0, errors=0, warnings≤threshold), NEEDS REVISION (exit 1, warnings>threshold but errors=0), or FAIL (exit 1, errors>0).

A backtick-wrapped filename or a recognized directory prefix (src/, tests/, lib/, etc.) in the skill/subagent body. Both validators require ≥3 such references to prove the artifact is codebase-specific rather than generic.

A phrase in the description that tells Claude when to invoke the skill/agent: 'use when', 'use for', 'trigger', or 'invoke'. Required field — missing it means Claude won't know the invocation condition.
A phrase in the description that tells Claude when NOT to use the skill/agent: 'do not use', 'don't use', 'not for', 'instead use', 'do not trigger'. Required to prevent over-triggering.

YAML multi-line string formats: `description: >` (folded, newlines become spaces) and `description: |` (literal, newlines preserved). The validator must parse both to correctly extract multi-line descriptions.
Naming convention enforced on skill folder names and subagent filenames: all lowercase, words separated by hyphens, no underscores or spaces. Folder name becomes the skill's name field.

mock_claude.bash mode where different agents get different mock responses based on schema filename or prompt content. Uses MOCK_CLAUDE_DISPATCH_DIR. Not used in tests/scripts/ but relevant to integration tests.
A subagent frontmatter field that grants the agent unrestricted tool access. validate-subagent.sh warns if present because it requires deliberate sandboxing justification.

gotchas.issue: mock_claude.bash is loaded in test_helper.bash's _common_setup but the mock binary is NOT automatically activated — you must explicitly call setup_mock_claude() in tests that need it. The scripts/ tests never call it because validators don't invoke claude. Forgetting this in a new integration test will cause real claude invocations.
gotchas.solution: Call setup_mock_claude() inside the test's setup() function (or at the start of the @test block) whenever the code under test calls claude -p. Check whether the code path hits claude before deciding.

gotchas.issue: validate-skill.sh derives the skill name from the parent folder of SKILL.md, not from the name: field in frontmatter. A dynamic test that creates $TEST_TMPDIR/SKILL.md (no subdirectory) will fail the folder-name check because the folder is the tmpdir hash.
gotchas.solution: Always create SKILL.md inside a named subdirectory: mkdir -p $TEST_TMPDIR/my-skill && cat > $TEST_TMPDIR/my-skill/SKILL.md.

gotchas.issue: The warning threshold differs between the two validators: skills fail if warnings > 3, subagents fail if warnings > 4. A test that expects assert_success may pass for subagents but fail for skills with the same fixture content.
gotchas.solution: Check the VERDICT and threshold logic in the specific validator before writing threshold-boundary tests. Don't assume both scripts share the same numeric threshold.

gotchas.issue: assert_output without --partial does an exact full-output match. Validator output includes dynamic metrics (word count, token estimate, path reference count) that vary by fixture content. Using assert_output 'VERDICT: PASS' will almost always fail.
gotchas.solution: Always use assert_output --partial 'VERDICT: PASS' (or whatever substring you're checking). Never use exact-match assert_output in these tests.

gotchas.issue: test_helper.bash sources all 8 lib/*.sh files on every setup(). If any lib file has a syntax error or sets a global bash option that breaks parsing (e.g. set -u exposed by an unset variable), all tests in the suite will fail at setup — not at the assertion — making the root cause hard to diagnose.
gotchas.solution: When a test suite fails at setup with a cryptic error, temporarily add 'set -x' to _common_setup and re-run to see which lib sourcing fails. Fix the lib, not the test.

gotchas.issue: The path-reference regex in validate-skill.sh matches backtick-wrapped content and specific directory prefixes. A skill body with file references using bold (**file.py**) or italic (*file.py*) formatting will NOT be counted — only backtick format (`file.py`) and recognized directory names match.
gotchas.solution: Always use backtick formatting for file and path references in SKILL.md bodies. Fixtures and tests that verify the reference count must use backtick format.

gotchas.issue: The tests/scripts/ suite has significantly fewer tests for validate-subagent.sh (6) than validate-skill.sh (21). Many subagent-specific checks (model field validation, bypassPermissions warning, tool-scope mismatch, overlap with built-in Explore agent) have no corresponding tests.
gotchas.solution: When adding new validation logic to validate-subagent.sh, add a corresponding test in validate_subagent.bats. The current low coverage means changes to the subagent validator could silently regress.

commands.purpose: Run only tests/scripts/ suite inside Docker
Run all bats suites (unit, scripts, integration, edge) inside Docker
Build the Docker test image locally (one-time, required before running tests)
Run just the skill validator tests directly in Docker
Run just the subagent validator tests directly in Docker
Manually invoke the skill validator against the golden fixture
Manually invoke the subagent validator against the golden fixture

skill_opportunities.name: add-validator-test
skill_opportunities.description: Scaffold a new @test block in validate_skill.bats or validate_subagent.bats for a given validation rule, including the correct heredoc fixture pattern, assert_success/assert_failure call, and assert_output --partial assertion.
workflow_steps: Identify which validator and which rule is being tested
Determine if the fixture should be a static file in tests/fixtures/ or a dynamic heredoc
Create the fixture with proper SKILL.md folder structure or flat .md file
Write @test block using run bash scripts/validate-X.sh <path>
Add assert_output --partial for the specific error/warning message text

skill_opportunities.name: expand-subagent-coverage
skill_opportunities.description: Add missing test cases to validate_subagent.bats for the currently untested checks: model field validation, bypassPermissions warning, tool-scope mismatch detection, and overlap-with-Explore-agent warning.
workflow_steps: Read scripts/validate-subagent.sh lines 98-170 to identify untested rules
For each untested rule, create a minimal heredoc fixture that triggers it
Write the @test block with assert_failure or assert_success plus assert_output --partial
Run make test-scripts to confirm each new test catches the intended behavior
